assertion.text:
Erdős Problem #935: declared status 'open'. Formalized: no. For any integer $n=\prod p^{k_p}$ let $Q_2(n)$ be the powerful part of $n$, so that\[Q_2(n) = \prod_{\substack{p\\ k_p\geq 2}}p^{k_p}.\]Is it true that, for every $\epsilon>0$ and $\ell\geq 1$, if $n$ is sufficiently large then\[Q_2(n(n+1)\cdots(n+\ell))<n^{2+\epsilon}?\]If $\ell\geq 2$ then is\[\limsup_{n\to \infty}\frac{Q_2(n(n+1)\cdots(n+\ell))}{n^2}\]infinite?

If $\ell\geq 2$ then is\[\lim_{n\to \infty}\frac{Q_2(n(n+1)\cdots(n+\ell))}{n^{\ell+1}}=0?\] Current best: A result of Mahler implies, for every $\ell\geq 1$,\[\limsup_{n\to \infty}\frac{Q_2(n(n+1)\cdots(n+\ell))}{n^2}\geq 1.\]All these questions can be asked replacing $Q_2$ by $Q_r$ for $r>2$, only keeping those prime powers with exponent $\geq r$. Prize: no. OEIS: A057521, A389244. Tags: number theory.
